questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Select the correct statement(s) from the following:
options: An equation that is dimensionally incorrect may be incorrect.
An equation that is dimensionally correct may be correct.
An equation that is dimensionally correct may be incorrect.
An equation that is dimensionally incorrect may be correct.
questions.solution:
According to the principle of homogeneity of dimensions, a valid physical equation must be dimensionally correct.

If an equation is dimensionally incorrect, it is always physically incorrect. Since it is definitely incorrect, the statement that it may be incorrect is logically true.

If an equation is dimensionally correct, it is not guaranteed to be physically correct because dimensional analysis provides no information about dimensionless constants or dimensionless variables. Thus, a dimensionally correct equation may be correct, or it may be incorrect.

A dimensionally incorrect equation can never be correct.

questions.subject: Physics
options: Every quantity may be represented dimensionally in terms of the base quantities.
A base quantity cannot be represented dimensionally using the rest of the base quantities.
The dimension of a base quantity in the other base quantities is always zero.
The dimension of a derived quantity is never zero in any of the base quantities.
questions.solution:
By definition, base quantities are fundamental and mutually independent. Therefore, a base quantity cannot be expressed in terms of other base quantities, which means its dimension in any other base quantity is always zero.

Every physical quantity can be expressed dimensionally as a product of powers of the base quantities. Even dimensionless quantities can be represented with zero powers of base quantities.

A derived quantity can have zero dimension in one or more base quantities. For example, velocity has the dimensional formula $[M^{0} L^{1} T^{-1}]$, where its dimension in mass is zero. Thus, the fourth statement is incorrect.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Work done by a force $= [MLT^{-2}][L] = [ML^2T^{-2}]$

Pressure $= \dfrac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}} = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$

Energy per unit volume $= \dfrac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Volume}} = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[L^3]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$

Linear momentum $= \text{Mass} \times \text{Velocity} = [M][LT^{-1}] = [MLT^{-1}]$

Both pressure and energy per unit volume correspond to the dimensions $ML^{-1}T^{-2}$.
